kanji.meaning_mnemonic: If you <radical>receive</radical> something edible, arrange it in a <radical>circle</radical> and <radical>boil</radical> it. That way, it’ll <kanji>ripen</kanji> more quickly.

The <radical>thread</radical> radical and <kanji>thread</kanji> are the same in looks as well as in meaning.

When a <radical>drunkard</radical> holds a <radical>sword</radical> it <kanji>becomes</kanji> something else. The drunkard doesn't become a swordsman, they're still just a drunkard. But the sword in their hands does.

You have some <radical>dirt</radical> next to a <radical>pool</radical> that has an extra <radical>drop</radical> of water in it because you need them to be <kanji>equal</kanji>. If there's too much dirt outside the pool and not enough water inside, it's gonna look weird. We can't have that.

Lying on the <radical>ground</radical> is something that looks just like the ground, the number <kanji>One</kanji>. Why is this One lying down? It's been shot by the number two. It's lying there, bleeding out and dying. The number One doesn't have long to live.
In the <radical>cage</radical> there are some <radical>legs</radical>. They are the legs attached to a <kanji>small animal</kanji>.

If you scrape a sharp <radical>stone</radical> against your <radical>skin</radical>, it will <kanji>tear</kanji>. The sharp stone tears your skin. It hurts!

The radical and the kanji are exactly the same, so this means <kanji>someone</kanji>.

You're known to get the ailment <radical>evening</radical> <radical>toe</radical>, which is when your toe catches on fire after getting really, really hot (imagine this happening, ouch!). The only way to fix it is to cool your toe down, and the best way to do that is to go <kanji>outside</kanji>.

If you play <radical>music</radical> with your <radical>fins</radical>, one <kanji>rule</kanji> is that you must be a fish. You can’t play music using fish fins that don’t belong to you. That’s the rule. And it’s also rude.

You have a <radical>private</radical> <radical>mouth</radical> that's a <kanji>machine</kanji>. But you have to keep your machine mouth private because it would scare all the non-machine people around you.

Under a <radical>canopy</radical>, there are two <radical>person</radical>s on the <radical>dirt</radical>. What do they do there? They <kanji>sit</kanji>. The people use the dirt under the canopy as a <kanji>seat</kanji> for their butts.

The <radical>power</radical> of your <radical>mouth</radical> lets you <kanji>add</kanji> words together to form a sentence. Marvel at the mighty additive power your mouth holds! You add words together in an amazing fashion!

They <radical>say</radical> that <radical>flowers</radical> held by a <radical>turkey</radical> on a <radical>stool</radical> are good to <kanji>defend</kanji> yourself with. Even better than a guard dog they say. People are so confused by the turkey sitting on a stool holding flowers that they just lose their will to fight. This turkey is great to defend yourself and your home.
If you <radical>say</radical> a new <radical>weapon</radical> idea out loud, then it will be <kanji>establish</kanji>ed immediately.

You need your <radical>net</radical> to be <radical>straight</radical> before you <kanji>put</kanji> it on the ground and try to lure something into it. You put your net there to catch something, but if it's twisted or tangled, it won't be very effective.

When you take your <radical>leader</radical>'s <radical>measurement</radical>s, you realize they're too short, so you <kanji>attach</kanji> things to them to make the measurements more impressive. Attach some heels to the shoes, then attach another head on top of theirs. There — perfect measurements thanks to these attachments.
You find a <radical>toe</radical> on the <radical>ground</radical>. It's weird, because it's <kanji>above</kanji> the ground, not where toes belong.
The kanji <kanji>two</kanji> and the radical <radical>two</radical> are the exact same things. Therefore, you know the kanji for two already!
Remember how the radical version of this kanji meant <radical>person</radical> because it looks like a person with their arms down and their legs spread out? Well this is a <kanji>person</kanji> too!
The <radical>eye</radical> radical and <kanji>eye</kanji> kanji are the same as each other, so now you know them both.

You have a scene going on inside your <radical>head</radical>. It is of a pair of <radical>horns</radical> on the <radical>ground</radical> atop a <radical>mountain</radical>. No. It is not just one pair of horns. It is many. So many that they create a giant <kanji>hill</kanji> atop the mountain, which is already pretty high.
The <radical>compare</radical> radical and the <kanji>compare</kanji> kanji are exactly the same! Huzzah!

Your new <radical>car</radical> doesn’t <radical>mix</radical> well with the other cars in your garage because its color is in stark <kanji>contrast</kanji> to the others. All of your other cars are pristine white in color, but this new car is a mix of lots of colors. It almost looks like a peacock or something it’s so colorful. Such a contrast.

Lying on the <radical>ground</radical> is something that looks just like the ground, the number <kanji>One</kanji>. Why is this One lying down? It's been shot by the number two. It's lying there, bleeding out and dying. The number One doesn't have long to live.

The kanji and the radical are the same, so remembering the meaning of this kanji is as simple as making sure you know the radical for <radical>book</radical> first! If you know the radical, you know this kanji means <kanji>book</kanji> as well.

Take note that the second, third, and fourth meanings of this kanji are <kanji>origin</kanji>, <kanji>real</kanji>, and <kanji>main</kanji>. You can figure this out by thinking about how books mainly have their origins in real life.

The <radical>fins</radical> that fell onto my <radical>sword</radical> were sliced into many <kanji>part</kanji>s. Following this logic, this also means <kanji>minute</kanji>, which is part of an hour, and <kanji>divide</kanji>, which is the action of splitting something into many parts.

Aim the <radical>scooter</radical>'s <radical>neck</radical> at the <kanji>road</kanji>! Scooters are only meant for driving on <kanji>street</kanji>s and <kanji>path</kanji>s, and if you go the wrong <kanji>way</kanji>, you'll probably break your own neck!
Remember how the radical version of this kanji meant <radical>person</radical> because it looks like a person with their arms down and their legs spread out? Well this is a <kanji>person</kanji> too!
The <radical>hand</radical> radical and the <kanji>hand</kanji> kanji are identical in looks and meaning! That should make things easy!
The <radical>origin</radical> radical and the <kanji>origin</kanji> kanji are the same!
Below the <radical>canopy</radical> of <radical>tree</radical>s is a <radical>demon</radical>. This demon is a <kanji>devil</kanji>.

When <radical>dawn</radical> reaches this <radical>village</radical> it increases in <kanji>quantity</kanji>. That's right. Every morning the <kanji>amount</kanji> of people or animals living here goes up by at least one.

Play your <radical>drum</radical> under the <radical>moon</radical> and your <radical>hair</radical> will <kanji>swell</kanji>! The more you play and the longer it's under the moon, the larger your hair will swell.
A <radical>tsunami</radical> came in and buried your <radical>flag</radical> so now you have to use a <radical>spoon</radical> and dig it out of the <kanji>mud</kanji>.

A <radical>tree</radical> in <radical>spring</radical> that's nice and <radical>dry</radical> will make a good <kanji>pole</kanji> or <kanji>rod</kanji>. Break it out of the ground and give it a swing to see what a nice, sturdy <kanji>stick</kanji> it is!

When a <radical>helicopter</radical> is piloted by a <radical>woman</radical>, it stirs an insatiable <kanji>need</kanji> in her. She doesn't know what it is. But she needs it. And that's why she's flying around looking for it!

If a <radical>grain</radical> seems extra <radical>heavy</radical>, you know it's a different <kanji>kind</kanji>. Not all <kanji>type</kanji>s of grains are the same. So when you feel the heavy type, you know it isn't the same as the light kind.

If someone is headed in your <radical>direction</radical> with a <radical>gun</radical>, nock an <radical>arrow</radical> in your bow and gather your <kanji>tribe</kanji> behind you because it’s about to get real! This man with a gun is headed your direction and he doesn’t even know what you and your tribe are gonna do to him. If he gets any closer, you’re likely to shoot that arrow right at him.

What is the <radical>evening</radical> <radical>mouth</radical>? Imagine a mouth that's up in the night sky. It's just there, talking, and only comes out at night. It only appears in one area of the world. (What area would you choose? Imagine yourself going to that place to see the "evening mouth.") And it appears only one month out of the year (more on this in a second). The "evening mouth" is the most famous thing in the _____ area (this is the area you choose). He's so <kanji>famous</kanji> that everybody knows his <kanji>name</kanji> ("Evening Mouth").

This kanji is made up of the ground radical (which also happens to be the kanji for "one") and the radical for two. Combine <radical>one</radical> and <radical>two</radical> together and what do you get? <kanji>Three</kanji>!
In the <radical>cage</radical> there are some <radical>legs</radical>. They are the legs attached to a <kanji>small animal</kanji>.
The <radical>eye</radical> radical and <kanji>eye</kanji> kanji are the same as each other, so now you know them both.

Your <radical>rice</radical> <radical>fortune</radical> is <kanji>sticky</kanji>. Rice is sticky. So is your rice fortune. Your fortune of rice is sticky because it is made up entirely of sticky rice.

A <radical>horn</radical>ed <radical>king</radical> on a <radical>slide</radical> catches your <radical>eye</radical> because you love what he's chosen to <kanji>wear</kanji>. You can't wait for him to <kanji>arrive</kanji> at the bottom of the slide so you can check out his outfit close-up.
The radical and the meaning of this kanji are the same. Both are <kanji>power</kanji>.
The <radical>fire</radical> radical and the <kanji>fire</kanji> kanji are the same as each other.
The <radical>hand</radical> radical and the <kanji>hand</kanji> kanji are identical in looks and meaning! That should make things easy!
The radical <radical>see</radical> and the kanji <kanji>see</kanji> are exactly the same!

There's a <radical>mouth</radical> inside a <radical>mouth</radical>. But if you look even closer (not actually, but we're pretending), you can see another mouth inside that mouth... and another one inside of that one! It keeps going and going. Can you count the number of <kanji>times</kanji> there's a mouth inside a mouth? This kanji also means <kanji>revolve</kanji> too. Think of each "time" a thing happens, it loops back around and comes back to the beginning, revolving around again another time.
The <radical>flag</radical> is <radical>old</radical> but that doesn't mean it's not still <kanji>alive</kanji>. This is a living flag, and it gets old just like other living things.

<radical>Dirt</radical> makes it <radical>easy</radical> to identify your <kanji>location</kanji>. Even if you add an extra line to mark the ground, it's still easy. Keep that in mind and you can go from <kanji>place</kanji> to place.
You're tearing this <radical>door</radical> down with an <radical>axe</radical>. You're absolutely crazy. "I HATE THIS <kanji>PLACE</kanji>!!" you keep yelling as you smash in the door.
The <radical>big</radical> radical and the <kanji>big</kanji> kanji are exactly the same!
The <radical>hat</radical> crowns the <radical>king</radical>, because <kanji>all</kanji> kings must have one.

meaning: to drive in (a nail, stake, etc.)

kanji.meaning_mnemonic: Take your <radical>fingers</radical> to the <radical>street</radical> to <kanji>hit</kanji> some people! Street fingers are for curling into a fist. And that street fist wants to do some hitting!
If you get on your <radical>scooter</radical> and <radical>enter</radical> somewhere, you’re going <kanji>into</kanji> that place.

If the <radical>sun</radical> starts to make a <radical>sound</radical>, you know it's about to go <kanji>dark</kanji>. The sun isn't supposed to make a sound, so if you can hear it, it's probably about to fizzle out or something. After it's gone, obviously everything will be pitch black. Get ready to be in the dark!
There's <radical>treasure</radical> in this <radical>tree</radical> so you take out your <radical>weapon</radical> and <kanji>kill</kanji> it.
The radical and the kanji are exactly the same, so this means <kanji>someone</kanji>.
Below the <radical>canopy</radical> of <radical>tree</radical>s is a <radical>demon</radical>. This demon is a <kanji>devil</kanji>.

A <radical>cow</radical> with <radical>wing</radical>s is flying in the air. What a strange <kanji>thing</kanji>! You've heard of flying pigs before, but cows? This is a new and strange thing indeed! Cows with wings! What a silly thing.
There's <radical>treasure</radical> in this <radical>tree</radical> so you take out your <radical>weapon</radical> and <kanji>kill</kanji> it.
